coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots/Ag3PO4 complex photocatalysts with enhanced visible light driven photocatalytic activity and stability
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Quanyi
Wang, Yunfang
Wang, Yawen
Zhang, Xiaochao
Duan, Donghong
Fan, Caimei
coredata.dc:description: Novel nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots/Ag3PO4 (NCQDs/Ag3PO4) complex photocatalysts were synthesized by a facile precipitation method at room temperature. The physical and chemical properties of Ag3PO4 and NCQDs/Ag3PO4 photocatalysts were detected through X-ray powder diffraction, field emission scanning electron microscopy, UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and electron spin resonance techniques. The as-prepared 3-NCQDs/Ag3PO4 composite exhibited much higher activity than the pure Ag3PO4 for eliminating methyl orange and bisphenol A solution under visible light (λ>420nm). Moreover, in the cyclic experiments, the 3-NCQDs/Ag3PO4 exhibited an excellent stability for the decolorization of methyl orange at some level. This suggested that NCQDs played an important role in the process of degradation. The function of NCQDs was discussed and a new mechanism was put forward for the degradation of methyl orange. The high activities and stability were attributed to the transfer of photogenerated charges through the vector of Ag3PO4 →NCQDs→Ag in the photocatalytic process, leading to effective charge separation of Ag3PO4.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NCQDs/Ag3PO4
Visible light
Photocatalytic
Degradation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Crystal structure of Ag3PO4 and NCQDs/Ag3PO4 composites
Possible mechanism of the photocatalytic reaction process

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of Ag3PO4 and NCQDs/Ag3PO4 composites with different NCQDs content.
XPS spectra of as-synthesized 3-NCQDs/Ag3PO4 composite: (a) survey, (b) Ag 3d, (c) P 2p, (d) C 1s, (e) O 1s and (f) N 1s.
FESEM images of Ag3PO4 (a), different magnification of 3-NCQDs/Ag3PO4 composite (b–d).
UV–vis spectra and band gaps of as-synthesized samples: (a) UV–vis spectra and (b) band gaps.
Photocatalytic performances of (a) MO and (b) BPA over NCQDs/Ag3PO4 composites under visible light irradiation.
Cycling runs for the photocatalytic degradation of MO of Ag3PO4 and 3-NCQDs/Ag3PO4 under visible light.

Scavenger experiments of reactive species during the photocatalytic degradation of MO over Ag3PO4 (a) and 3-NCQDs/Ag3PO4 sample (b) under visible light irradiation. ESR spectra of Ag3PO4 and 3-NCQDs/Ag3PO4 sample under dark and visible light irradiation (c), XRD patterns of Ag3PO4 and 3-NCQDs/Ag3PO4 photocatalysts after the degradation experiments (d).
The proposed photocatalytic mechanism diagram of NCQDs/Ag3PO4 composites under visible light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots/Ag3PO4 complex photocatalysts with enhanced visible light driven photocatalytic activity and stability

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Taiyuan University of Technology, Taiyuan 030024, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The excellent visible light photocatalytic activity and stability of NCQDs/Ag3PO4 photocatalysts was mainly attributed to the more effective separation rate of electron-hole.

Novel nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots/Ag3PO4 (NCQDs/Ag3PO4) complex photocatalysts were synthesized by a facile precipitation method at room temperature. The physical and chemical properties of Ag3PO4 and NCQDs/Ag3PO4 photocatalysts were detected through X-ray powder diffraction, field emission scanning electron microscopy, UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and electron spin resonance techniques. The as-prepared 3-NCQDs/Ag3PO4 composite exhibited much higher activity than the pure Ag3PO4 for eliminating methyl orange and bisphenol A solution under visible light (λ>420nm). Moreover, in the cyclic experiments, the 3-NCQDs/Ag3PO4 exhibited an excellent stability for the decolorization of methyl orange at some level. This suggested that NCQDs played an important role in the process of degradation. The function of NCQDs was discussed and a new mechanism was put forward for the degradation of methyl orange. The high activities and stability were attributed to the transfer of photogenerated charges through the vector of Ag3PO4 →NCQDs→Ag in the photocatalytic process, leading to effective charge separation of Ag3PO4.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants assisted by a semiconductor has attracted extensive attention of both the public and research groups for this reason that it provides a potentially promising approach to face the challenges of environmental pollution and energy crises [1]. Unfortunately, most widely employed semiconductor photocatalysts are only active under UV-light irradiation, such as TiO2 that only accounts for 4% of the total sunlight and thus significantly limits its practical applications in the utilizing of solar light [2–4]. Particular interest is focused on the development of new and more efficient visible light driven photocatalysts which are strongly desired for the effective harvest of solar energy is a major challenge in this field.

Most recently, it has been reported that it has been reported that Ag3PO4 is a visible light responsive photocatalyst which can achieve high quantum efficiency (up to 90%) for O2 evolution from water as well as great photo-decomposition of organic compounds. However, the fact that Ag3PO4 is slightly soluble in aqueous solution and the photocorrosion is very serious [5–7], prevents its wide use in the environmental pollution field. Synthesis of Ag3PO4-based composite photocatalysts can suppress the recombination of photo-generated charge carriers. For example, AgI/Ag3PO4 [8–10], Ag3PO4/Co3O4 [11], Ag3PO4/BiOBr [12] and Ag3PO4/g-C3N4 [13–16] composite photocatalysts can accelerate the separation of photo-generated charge carriers, which enhance not only Ag3PO4 photocatalytic activity but also its stability [17]. Because of the large-size materials can hardly construct perfect contact interfaces between each other; the contact interfaces of these composite systems are insufficient and incompact. In order to make up for the shortage, small-sized quantum dots materials that are the uniform distribution can be utilized to form the sufficient and compact contact interfaces in the composite photocatalyst systems [18].

In recent years, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a novel class of nanocarbons with sizes below 10nm, CQDs have aroused much attention due to their unique and excellent properties, such as robust chemical inertness, low toxicity, superior stability against photobleaching and wavelength dependent luminescence emission [19,20]. CQDs possess amorphous nanocrystalline cores with predominantly graphitic or turbostratic carbon (sp2 carbon) or graphene and graphene oxide sheets fused by diamond-like sp3 hybridized carbon insertions, which make carbon quantum dots have the abilities of transferring and reserving electrons [21]. Therefore, the CQDs have been applied to the photocatalysis system so as to improve the photocatalytic activity and stability both for hydrogen generation and pollutant removal, such as CQDs/TiO2 [22–24], CQDs/BiOX [25,26], and CQDs/Ag3PO4 [27]. However, it is still worth waiting for improving the better performance of CQDs by suitable methods so as to acquire satisfactory photocatalytic activity and stability of CQDs-based materials. The nitrogen doping can efficiently induce charge delocalization and tune the work function of carbon, which is effective to change the electronic characteristics and energy states of CQDs. Moreover, the electron transfer capability of carbon materials can be efficiently promoted [28]. Therefore, the desirable photocatalytic performance is expected if NCQDs are constructed and introduced to the semiconductor system by a feasible approach.

In this work, novel NCQDs/Ag3PO4 composite photocatalysts have been first prepared via a facile precipitation method. The morphologies, optical properties and photocatalytic properties are investigated in detail. The photocatalytic activities of NCQDs/Ag3PO4 complex materials are evaluated sufficiently by degrading of two different kinds of organic pollutants under visible light irradiation. The reason for the enhancement of the photocatalytic activity and stability are also discussed in detail. The possible mechanism of the photocatalytic degradation in this system is studied by using free radicals trapping experiments and electron spin resonance (ESR) analysis.

Silver nitrate (AgNO3, ⩾99.8%), was analytical reagents (A.R.) and purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd in Shanghai. Sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4, ⩾99%), ethylenediamine (C2H8N2, ⩾99%) and d-Glucose were analytical reagents (A.R.) and purchased from Tianjin Kermel Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All chemicals were analytical grade and used without further purification.

NCQDs were prepared through a simple hydrothermal synthesis method. In a typical procedure, 3.96g of d-Glucose was dissolved in 40mL deionized water, followed by the addition of 2.67mL ethylenediamine. Then, the above solution was transferred into a capacity of 100mL sealed Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave which was heated to 180°C and kept for 11h under autogenous pressure. After cooled to room temperature, the solution was filtered with a 0.22μm polyethersulfone membrane to remove the larger carbon nanoparticles. After that, the NCQDs were further dialyzed in a dialysis bag (cutoff molecular weight: 500Da) for 3days. At last, the NCQDs powder was obtained by freeze drying.

The NCQDs/Ag3PO4 composites were synthesized by a facile precipitation method at room temperature. 1.213g AgNO3 was dissolved in 20mL deionized water and a certain amount of NCQDs (0.1g/L, dispersed in distilled water) was added to the AgNO3 solution. Then, NaH2PO4 solution was prepared by dissolving 0.746g NaH2PO4 in 20mL distilled water and added to the above solution dropwise under stirring, afterthat the resulting mixture was subjected to be constant magnetic stirring at room temperature for 30min in the dark. The obtained NCQDs/Ag3PO4 samples were washed, centrifuged several times with deionized water, and dried at 60°C for 6h. Finally, the NCQDs/Ag3PO4 sample was obtained. The process is shown in Scheme 1 . The Ag3PO4 catalysts were simply prepared in the process of synthesis of water instead of NCQDs solution. The NCQDs/Ag3PO4 composites prepared by changing the amount of NCQDs solution of 1, 3, and 5mL were labeled as 1-NCQDs/Ag3PO4, 3-NCQDs/Ag3PO4, and 5-NCQDs/Ag3PO4, respectively.

The crystal structures and phase states of NCQDs/Ag3PO4 composites were determined by X-ray diffractometry (XRD) using D/max-2500 diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (λ=0.15406nm) at a scanning rate of 0.08°/s and the scanning range was 10–80°. The morphologies of samples were observed with a field-emission scanning electron microscopy (JSM-7001F). UV–vis diffused reflectance spectra (DRS) of the samples were obtained for the dry-pressed film samples using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UH4150, Hitachi, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on a Thermo ESCALAB 250 Xi XPS system with an Al Ka (hv=1486.6eV) 150W, 500μm beam spot source. Room temperature electron spin resonant (ESR) was recorded on a Bruker model JES-FA200 spectroscopy with the signals of radical spin-trapped by DMPO in water and methanol.

The photocatalytic activities of Ag3PO4 and NCQDs/Ag3PO4 complex materials were evaluated sufficiently by degrading of methyl orange (MO) or bisphenol A (BPA) solution under visible light irradiation. In the typical photocatalytic degradation experiment process, the photocatalyst (100mg) was added into MO solution (100mL, 10mg/L) or BPA solution (100mL, 15mg/L) to produce a suspension at room temperature with the magnetic stirring and ensure to establish the adsorption-desorption equilibrium. The 500W xenon lame was used as the visible light source with filter glasses (λ=420nm) as the simulated sunlight. In a certain time, a 3mL sample of the reaction suspension was sampled and centrifuged at 8000rpm for 3min. The residual pollution concentration was collected and analyzed on the UV–vis spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Crystal structure of Ag3PO4 and NCQDs/Ag3PO4 composites
ce:section.ce:para: The crystal structure and phase composition of the as-prepared photocatalysts were analyzed by XRD. Fig. 1 displays the XRD patterns of Ag3PO4 and different compound ratio of NCQDs/Ag3PO4. It can be seen that all the diffraction peaks of Ag3PO4 and NCQDs/Ag3PO4 composites with various NCQDs contents match well with the cubic structure of Ag3PO4 (JCPDS No. 06-0505), and the crystallization degree is very good [7,27]. The introduction of NCQDs has no obvious effect on the crystallinity and purity of composite materials [29]. Moreover, the diffraction peak intensity of carbon at 2θ=25.6° is too weak to be observed, the reason is that the quantity of NCQDs is small and similar results can also be found in the previous reports [27,28,30].

The morphological and microstructural details of the products were examined by the scanning electron microscopy (SEM). As shown in Fig.3 a, the SEM image reveals that the obtained Ag3PO4 photocatalyst has the spherical morphology with a size of about 2–3μm. Fig.3b–d displays the SEM images of 3-NCQDs/Ag3PO4 complex photocatalyst and compared with the Ag3PO4 photocatalyst, some particles with the regular polyhedron morphology can be observed. Moreover, many small particles attached on the surface of 3-NCQDs/Ag3PO4 complex photocatalyst are likely to be the Ag nanoparticles.

UV–vis absorption spectra of Ag3PO4 and the 3-NCQDs/Ag3PO4 composite were recorded, as shown in Fig.4 a. With the addition of NCQDs, there is an enhanced absorbance for 3-NCQDs/Ag3PO4 material in the region ranging from 520 to 800nm. It is well known that the optical absorption near the band edge follows the formula (αhν)1/2 =A (hν−Eg), where α, h, ν, Eg, and A are absorption coefficient, Planck constant, light frequency, band gap, and a constant, respectively [11].

According to the plot of (αhν)1/2 versus the band gap energy (hν) is shown in Fig.4b. The band gap energies of Ag3PO4 and 3-NCQDs/Ag3PO4 have been calculated to be 2.32 and 2.27eV, respectively. Furthermore, the conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB) of the semiconductor can be calculated according to the empirical formula [36]: ECB =χ−Ee −0.5Eg, EVB =ECB +Eg. In the above formula, χ represents the electronegativity of the semiconductor, so the χ value for Ag3PO4 is 5.96eV; Ee is the energy of free electrons on the hydrogen scale (about 4.5eV). Thus, the ECB and EVB of Ag3PO4 are calculated to be +0.30eV and +2.62eV, respectively.

To compare the photocatalytic activities of Ag3PO4 and different compound ratio of NCQDs/Ag3PO4, a series of photocatalytic degradation experiments were performed using methyl orange (MO) as a model pollutant under visible light irradiation. MO was only slightly degraded in the absence of catalyst (blank reaction in Fig.5 a), indicating that MO was a stable molecule under visible light irradiation and the blank photolytic degradation degree was negligible. Nearly 98% of MO was degraded with 18min irradiation in the presence of 3-NCQDs/Ag3PO4 complex photocatalyst, indicating its excellent photocatalystic activity. Only 76% MO was degraded by pure Ag3PO4 under visible light irradiation in 18min. These results indicated that 3-NCQDs/Ag3PO4 composite had the highest photocatalystic activity than pure Ag3PO4 and the others compound ratio of NCQDs/Ag3PO4.

The degradation of bisphenol A (BPA) was implemented to further evaluate the photocatalytic performance of NCQDs/Ag3PO4 composites under visible light irradiation. As can be seen from Fig.5b, BPA was degraded 52% over Ag3PO4 under visible light irradiation for 36min, while the degradation ratio was significantly increased to 90% over the 3-NCQDs/Ag3PO4 composite photocatalyst under the same conditions. These results indicated that NCQDs/Ag3PO4 could be used for the removal of BPA and the decomposition of BPA molecules resulted from photocatalytic degradation instead of physical adsorption. In addition, as a representative photocatalyst, an examination to the recycled photocatalytic activity of Ag3PO4 and 3-NCQDs/Ag3PO4 was also carried out, and the experimental results were displayed in Fig. 6 . In the cyclic MO tests, the 3-NCQDs/Ag3PO4 exhibited an excellent stability with an average MO removal efficiency, which indicated good photocatalytic stability of the 3-NCQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid composites at some level.

In order to further clarify the possible reaction mechanism, reactive oxygen species trapping tests were designed to study the main reactive oxygen species in the photocatalytic process of Ag3PO4 and 3-NCQDs/Ag3PO4 under visible light as shown in Fig.7 a and b. We detected the oxidative species in the catalytic systems of Ag3PO4 and 3-NCQDs/Ag3PO4 under visible light by adding different scavengers p-benzoquinone (PBQ) for
O2 −, ammonium oxalate (AO) for h+, and isopropyle alcohol (IPA) for
OH. The addition of 1mM isopropyle alcohol (IPA) scavenger had weak inhibition efficiency of 4.0% and 6.1% of MO degradation for Ag3PO4 and 3-NCQDs/Ag3PO4, respectively, indicating that
OH had negligibly small effect on the photocatalytic degradation of MO process. There were 61.4% and 58.8% depressions for AO addition on Ag3PO4 and 3-NCQDs/Ag3PO4, which revealed the active species of h+ play an important role in the photocatalytic process. For PBQ addition, 44.2% and 55.2% depressions were shown on Ag3PO4 and 3-NCQDs/Ag3PO4, respectively. Therefore, it could be concluded that h+ and
O2 − are the key active species of Ag3PO4 and 3-NCQDs/Ag3PO4 in aqueous solution under visible light irradiation. Comparatively speaking, the generation of
O2 − on the surface of 3-NCQDs/Ag3PO4 was more than that of the Ag3PO4.

Moreover, the ESR spin-trap tests with 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) were performed, as shown in Fig.7c. There were no ESR signal could be observed in the dark for both the 3-NCQDs/Ag3PO4 and pure Ag3PO4 sample. However, the DMPO −
O2 − species were detected in methanol dispersions of two samples where six characteristic peaks of the DMPO −
O2 − adducts were observed under visible light irradiation. Furthermore, 3-NCQDs/Ag3PO4 showed the stronger signals of
O2 − than that of Ag3PO4 under visible light irradiation, indicating that more
O2 − was generated during the irradiation of 3-NCQDs/Ag3PO4 at room temperature, which could be concluded that the introduction of NCQDs produced more
O2 − on the surface of Ag3PO4 photocatalyst [34,37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanism of the photocatalytic reaction process
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the results of the above experiments and characterization, a likely mechanism for the photocatalytic degradation by NCQDs/Ag3PO4 composites is proposed and the schematic illustration for the mechanism has been summarized in Scheme 2 . As we known, the Ag3PO4 is excited by light with wavelength shorter than 530nm to generate photogenerated electron-hole pairs [34]. In the process of photocatalytic, the surface of the pure Ag3PO4 will continuously produce elemental silver, which severely reduces the photocatalytic activity and stability of the silver phosphate. In order to verify the generation of Ag in the photocatalytic process, we examined the XRD patterns of pure Ag3PO4 and 3-NCQDs/Ag3PO4 photocatalyst after the degradation experiments of methyl orange. As shown in Fig. 7d, it can be clearly seen that obvious diffraction peaks of metallic silver appeared in the XRD pattern of pure Ag3PO4 and 3-NCQDs/Ag3PO4 photocatalyst, indicating that partial Ag3PO4 reduction into metallic Ag particles had taken place during the MO degradation process. Therefore, the Ag nanoparticles are introduced into the NCQDs/Ag3PO4 composite photocatalyst system.

Due to the fact that NCQDs have a lower unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) level than Ag3PO4, moreover, the CB of Ag3PO4 is located at more positive level than Fermi lever of Ag nanoparticles (0.4V vs NHE) [27,28]. Therefore, the electrons on the surface of Ag3PO4 are transferred through the vector of Ag3PO4 →NCQDs→Ag in the photocatalytic process [30,38], which form the Schottky junction in the NCQDs/Ag3PO4 composite material system and generate an electromagnetic field that improve the efficiency of electron hole pair separation. The electron-hole pairs are easily separated under the influence of the surface potential and travel to the surface of Ag3PO4, where holes can be directly degraded the MO solution and electrons will be further involved in chemical reactions. Moreover, the redox potential of O2/
O2 − is −0.13eV, therefore the CB electrons of Ag3PO4 cannot reduce the
O2 −. Oxygen radicals would be formed by the combination of electrons with O2 adsorbed on the surfaces of Ag which may cause degradation of MO [18,39]. In a word, these above aspects are together responsible for the enhanced photocatalytic activity and good stability of NCQDs/Ag3PO4 complex photocatalyst is compared to pure Ag3PO4 at some level.

An efficient NCQDs/Ag3PO4 hybrid composite for organic dye degradation was fabricated via a facile precipitation method at room temperature. Compared with pure Ag3PO4, the NCQDs/Ag3PO4 complex photocatalyst shows an excellent photocatalytic degradation activity and stability. The superior photoreactivity of the composite may be ascribed to the photoinduced electron transfer properties of NCQDs and the SPR effect of Ag nanopairticles. Here, it is expected that the high photocatalytic performances of NCQDs/Ag3PO4 may provide a new strategy in the design of new catalyst design towards new energy sources, green chemistry, and environmental issues.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Youth National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21206105) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21176168).
